examples.title: Basic Thread Creation with Pthreads
examples.description: Demonstrates creating threads, passing arguments, and joining threads in C using POSIX threads
examples.explanation: This program creates 3 threads using pthread_create(). Each thread executes the thread_function which prints its number and thread ID. The main thread uses pthread_join() to wait for all child threads to complete before exiting. The pthread argument is used to pass data to each thread.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for thread stacks plus thread ID storage

examples.title: Mutex Lock for Critical Section
examples.description: Demonstrates using mutex locks to protect shared counter from race conditions
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates mutex usage to prevent race conditions. Five threads each increment a shared counter 1000 times. Without mutex locks, the final value would be less than 5000 due to race conditions. With mutex, the critical section is protected ensuring correct final count.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * m) for n threads each performing m operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for shared data plus O(n) for thread storage

examples.title: Thread Creation in Java
examples.description: Demonstrates two methods of creating threads in Java - using Thread class and Runnable interface
examples.explanation: Java provides two ways to create threads: (1) Extending Thread class - simple but prevents class from extending other classes. (2) Implementing Runnable interface - preferred as Java supports single inheritance. The start() method actually creates and runs the thread, not the run() method directly.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for thread creation, O(n) for execution
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for task, O(n) for thread stack allocation

examples.title: Threading in Python
examples.description: Demonstrates creating and managing threads using Python's threading module with synchronization
examples.code:
import threading
import time

# Shared resource
counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()

def increment_counter(thread_name, iterations):
    global counter
    for i in range(iterations):
        with lock:  # Acquire lock, execute, release lock
            counter += 1
            print(f"{thread_name}: counter = {counter}")
        time.sleep(0.001)  # Simulate some work

def thread_worker(name, delay):
    print(f"Thread {name} starting")
    time.sleep(delay)
    print(f"Thread {name} finishing")

# Create threads
threads = []
t1 = threading.Thread(target=increment_counter, args=("Thread-1", 10))
t2 = threading.Thread(target=increment_counter, args=("Thread-2", 10))
t3 = threading.Thread(target=thread_worker, args=("Worker-1", 1))

# Start threads
t1.start()
t2.start()
t3.start()

# Wait for completion
t1.join()
t2.join()
t3.join()

print(f"Final counter value: {counter}")
print("All threads completed")

# Demonstrate thread properties
print(f"\nMain thread: {threading.main_thread().name}")
print(f"Current thread: {threading.current_thread().name}")
print(f"Active threads: {threading.active_count()}")
examples.explanation: Python's threading module provides Thread class for creating threads. The 'with lock' statement is context manager syntax that ensures proper lock acquisition and release. This example demonstrates: (1) thread creation with target function, (2) passing arguments, (3) lock synchronization for shared data, (4) join() for waiting, (5) thread properties access.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n iterations per thread
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for thread objects plus shared counter
